items.evidence_text:
Client 1: Yep—let’s quantify it.
Advisor: Great. Starting with household income—ballpark is fine, but what do you have as your total gross income per year for the two of you combined?
Client 1: Combined gross is 173100 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—William, what makes up your side versus Elizabeth’s? Salary, bonus, business income, rental—whatever applies.
Client 1: Mine’s a mix. There’s business income that runs weekly—annualized it’s 74800. Then I’ve got a monthly bonus that totals 37350 for the year, and an annual bonus of 52600.

Client 1: Yeah—those are both 0 right now. The ad hoc bonus is 0 and the salary line is 0.
Advisor: Okay. Switching to spending—what are you seeing as your total monthly expenses, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, groceries, insurance, travel—everything.
Client 2: Roughly eight-ish. The number we use is 7850 per month.
Advisor: Thanks. Now debt—do you have any loans or cards with a regular payment we should bake in?
Client 1: Just one loan. Balance is 3100.

Client 1: Combined gross is 173100 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—William, what makes up your side versus Elizabeth’s? Salary, bonus, business income, rental—whatever applies.
Client 1: Mine’s a mix. There’s business income that runs weekly—annualized it’s 74800. Then I’ve got a monthly bonus that totals 37350 for the year, and an annual bonus of 52600.
Client 2: And I’m employed too—my gross is 59200 annually. Plus we have rental income coming in monthly—annual total is 8400.
Advisor: Perfect. And I heard you say there’s also an ad hoc bonus line and a salary line that are currently zero—am I hearing that right?

Advisor: Got it—thank you. So total monthly debt payments, just that one, is 350. Now let’s talk assets—what you’ve accumulated and where it sits.
Advisor: When you think about investable assets—so not the house—what’s the total you’re tracking across accounts?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 662950.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s break that down so I understand what’s joint versus individual. Do you have a joint taxable brokerage account?
Client 1: Yes—joint taxable brokerage is 323600.

Client 1: Yes, that one is 21950.
Advisor: And then the home—what are you using as the current value of your primary residence?
Client 2: We use 446250 for the house value.
Advisor: Helpful. On protection—life insurance, disability, long-term care—anything in place that you want us to factor in today?
Client 1: Nothing formal to list right now—no policies we’re tracking.

Advisor: Hi William, hi Elizabeth—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the numbers, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m William. We’re trying to sanity-check retirement timing and taxes—like, are we actually on track or just assuming we are.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth. I mostly want to know what we’d need to change month to month, if anything, without it feeling scary.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s anchor on the basics first—income, spending, what you’ve built up, and any debt. Then we can talk options. Sound okay?
Client 1: Yep—let’s quantify it.

Client 2: MA.
Advisor: Got it—Massachusetts. And tax-wise, what bracket are we planning around right now? Just the band is fine.
Client 1: 32%.
Advisor: Okay. And last admin-ish thing: how did you come to us—what’s the relationship channel you want this to be under? Like, are we acting as your RIA, or is this through a brokerage arrangement?
Client 2: RIA (registered investment advisor).

Advisor: Hi William, hi Elizabeth—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the numbers, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m William. We’re trying to sanity-check retirement timing and taxes—like, are we actually on track or just assuming we are.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth. I mostly want to know what we’d need to change month to month, if anything, without it feeling scary.

Advisor: Great. On protection, you said nothing formal to list—so no life insurance, disability coverage, or long-term care policies we’re tracking right now?
Client 2: Right—nothing we’re tracking.
Advisor: Okay. And on assets—just to keep the categories clean—your investable accounts were the joint taxable brokerage, Elizabeth’s retirement account at the bank, the joint retirement-platform account, and William’s private markets holding. Anything else material we didn’t mention?
Client 1: No, that’s the full list we’re using.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Next we’ll talk goals and timing, but I wanted to make sure the debt and home pieces were nailed down first.

Advisor: Okay. Dependents—any kids or anyone financially dependent on you right now?
Client 2: No—number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Got it. And then risk—when markets get choppy, how do you want the portfolio positioned? More conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client 1: Aggressive.
Advisor: Okay—thanks, William. And Elizabeth, when you say aggressive, does that feel right to you too, or do you want some guardrails around cash and drawdowns?

Advisor: Alright—before we get into retirement timing, I want to confirm a few household basics so I’m not building the plan on assumptions. First one: you’re in the US, correct?
Client 1: Yes, US.
Advisor: Great. And marital status—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, married.
Advisor: Okay. Dependents—any kids or anyone financially dependent on you right now?

Client 1: Operations. I’m in operations.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And then income: earlier we had household gross at 173100 a year, but I want to anchor your base number. William, what’s your gross annual income before bonuses and the business income pieces—what do you consider your gross annual income?
Client 1: My gross annual income is 113900.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. And that 113900 is the steady gross pay you’re counting on, then we layer the other items like the weekly business income and the bonuses on top—fair?
Client 1: Yeah, that’s fair—113900 is the core number for me.

Advisor: Any private investments—private markets—held on a retirement platform in your name, William?
Client 1: Yes, that one is 21950.
Advisor: And then the home—what are you using as the current value of your primary residence?
Client 2: We use 446250 for the house value.
Advisor: Helpful. On protection—life insurance, disability, long-term care—anything in place that you want us to factor in today?

Client 1: Yep—let’s quantify it.
